subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Year 7! This unit opens the door to a bigger world of numbers than the one you have used so far. Until now, almost every number you counted with was a positive whole number: the goats coming home at dusk, the shillings in your pocket at the Hargeisa market, the pages left in a book. Positive numbers are perfect for counting things you can hold. But the real world also contains ideas that go below zero – a temperature five degrees below freezing, a bank account overdrawn by twenty dollars, a diver forty metres beneath the surface of the sea near Berbera. To describe these, you need integers.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Understand that integers include positive whole numbers, negative whole numbers, and zero, and place them correctly on a number line.
Compare and order integers, and explain why -10 is less than -5 even though 10 is greater than 5.
Find the opposite of an integer and the absolute value of an integer.
Add and subtract integers using the number line and the sign rules, including subtracting a negative.
Multiply and divide integers using the sign rules, and explain *why* a negative times a negative is positive.
Find multiples and the lowest common multiple (LCM) of two or more numbers.
Find factors and the highest common factor (HCF) of two or more numbers.
Calculate square roots and cube roots of perfect squares and cubes, and estimate other roots.
Use the correct order of operations with powers, roots and integers, and solve real-world problems.

concepts.title: Integers and the Number Line
concepts.explanation: The number line is your most powerful tool for understanding integers. Picture a straight horizontal line stretching forever in both directions, with zero at the centre. To the right of zero sit the positive integers (1, 2, 3, 4, …). To the left of zero sit the negative integers (-1, -2, -3, -4, …). Every integer has exactly one home on this line. The number line makes the order of integers obvious. As you move from left to right, numbers always increase. As you move from right to left, they decrease. This single…
concepts.example: The number line also shows opposites. Every positive integer has a matching negative integer the same distance from zero but on the other side. For example, 3 and -3 are opposites because both are exactly 3 units from…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting Integers
concepts.explanation: Adding and subtracting integers can feel tricky at first, but two clear methods will carry you through every problem: the number-line method and the sign rules. Learn both, because each helps you understand the other. When two integers have the same sign, add their absolute values and keep the shared sign. If both are positive, this is ordinary addition: 5 + 3 = 8. If both are negative, add the sizes and keep the minus: (-5) + (-3) = -8. Think of debt: if Hassan owes 5 shillings and then borrows another 3, he owes…
concepts.example: When the signs are different, subtract the smaller absolute value from the larger, and keep the sign of the number with the larger absolute value. For 7 + (-3), the sizes are 7 and 3; subtract to get 4, and since 7 is…

concepts.title: Multiplying and Dividing Integers
concepts.explanation: Multiplying and dividing integers follow one neat pattern about signs, plus a good reason why the pattern is true. Once you understand it, these operations become the easiest part of the unit. The sign rules are short: same signs give a positive result, different signs give a negative result. For multiplication that means positive × positive = positive (3 × 4 = 12), negative × negative = positive ((-3) × (-4) = 12), positive × negative = negative (3 × (-4) = -12), and negative × positive = negative ((-3) × 4 =…
concepts.example: But why does a negative times a negative make a positive? Memorising a rule is not the same as understanding it. Think of multiplication as repeated addition. 3 × 4 means 'add 4, three times': 4 + 4 + 4 = 12. Now 3 ×…

concepts.title: Multiples and the Lowest Common Multiple (LCM)
concepts.explanation: A multiple of a number is what you get when you multiply that number by an integer. The multiples of 6 are 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36, … found by multiplying 6 by 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and so on. Because you can always multiply by a larger integer, the list of multiples of any number goes on forever. The lowest common multiple, or LCM, of two or more numbers is the smallest positive number that is a multiple of all of them. The LCM is the tool you use to add or subtract fractions with different denominators, because it…
concepts.example: The listing method works well for small numbers. Write out the multiples of each number until you find the smallest one they share. To find the LCM of 4 and 6: the multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, … and the…

concepts.title: Factors and the Highest Common Factor (HCF)
concepts.explanation: A factor of a number is any integer that divides into it exactly, with no remainder. The factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 and 12, because each of these divides 12 evenly. Factors are always less than or equal to the number itself, and every number has 1 and itself as factors. The highest common factor, or HCF (also called the greatest common divisor), is the largest integer that divides two or more numbers exactly. It is the tool you use to simplify fractions and to split quantities into the largest equal groups.…
concepts.example: The listing method: write all the factors of each number and pick the largest they share. For 18 and 24, the factors of 18 are 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18 and the factors of 24 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24. The common factors are…

concepts.title: Tests for Divisibility
concepts.explanation: Divisibility tests are shortcuts that tell you whether one number divides exactly into another, without doing the full division. They save time when finding factors, simplifying fractions, and building prime factorisations. Here are the tests you need for this unit. Divisible by 2: the number ends in an even digit (0, 2, 4, 6 or 8).
concepts.example: Divisible by 3: the sum of the digits is divisible by 3. For 2,847 the digits sum to 2+8+4+7 = 21, and 21 is divisible by 3, so 2,847 is too.

methods.title: How to find the LCM
steps: Write each number as a product of prime factors.
Take every prime that appears, at its highest power in any number.
Multiply these together – that is the LCM.

methods.title: How to find the HCF
steps: Write each number as a product of prime factors.
Take only the primes that are shared, at their lowest power.
Multiply these together – that is the HCF.

methods.title: For a cube root, ask: what number times itself three times gives this value?
steps: If it is not a perfect square or cube, estimate using the nearest perfect ones.
Check the for a cube root, ask: what number times itself three times gives this value? result carefully.
Check the for a cube root, ask: what number times itself three times gives this value? result carefully.

methods.title: Temperature change
methods.example: At 6 a.m. the temperature was -12°C. It rose 3°C each hour until noon (6 hours), then fell 2°C each hour until 6 p.m. (6 hours). Find the temperature at 6 p.m.
steps: Solution: Rise over 6 hours: 6 × 3 = 18, so at noon the temperature is -12 + 18 = 6°C.
Fall over 6 hours: 6 × 2 = 12, so at 6 p.m.

methods.title: LCM by prime factorisation
steps: Solution: 15 = 3 × 5 and 20 = 2² × 5.
Take each prime at its highest power: 2², 3 and 5.

methods.title: HCF by prime factorisation
steps: Solution: 24 = 2³ × 3 and 36 = 2² × 3².
Take only the shared primes at their lowest power: 2² and 3.
The HCF is 2² × 3 = 4 × 3 = 12.

workedExamples.title: Order a set of integers
workedExamples.solution: Solution: On the number line, the further left, the smaller. The negatives come first, and among negatives a larger size means smaller value, so -12 < -3. Then comes 0, then the positives. The order is -12, -3, 0, +5, +8.

workedExamples.title: Subtract a negative
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtracting a negative is adding the opposite: (-12) - (-5) = (-12) + (+5). Different signs, so subtract sizes 12 and 5 to get 7, and keep the sign of the larger size (12, negative). The answer is -7.

workedExamples.title: A chain of multiplications
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count the negative signs: there are two, an even number, so the result is positive. Multiply the sizes: 7 × 4 × 2 = 56. The answer is +56. Check by steps: (-7) × 4 = -28, then -28 × (-2) = +56.

workedExamples.title: Temperature change
workedExamples.prompt: At 6 a.m. the temperature was -12°C. It rose 3°C each hour until noon (6 hours), then fell 2°C each hour until 6 p.m. (6 hours). Find the temperature at 6 p.m.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Rise over 6 hours: 6 × 3 = 18, so at noon the temperature is -12 + 18 = 6°C. Fall over 6 hours: 6 × 2 = 12, so at 6 p.m. it is 6 - 12 = -6°C.

workedExamples.title: LCM by prime factorisation
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 15 = 3 × 5 and 20 = 2² × 5. Take each prime at its highest power: 2², 3 and 5. The LCM is 2² × 3 × 5 = 4 × 15 = 60.

workedExamples.title: HCF by prime factorisation
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 24 = 2³ × 3 and 36 = 2² × 3². Take only the shared primes at their lowest power: 2² and 3. The HCF is 2² × 3 = 4 × 3 = 12.

workedExamples.title: A divisibility check
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A number is divisible by 6 if it is divisible by both 2 and 3. It ends in 4, an even digit, so it passes the test for 2. Its digits sum to 7+5+2+4 = 18, which is divisible by 3, so it passes the test for 3. Since it passes both, 7,524 is divisible by 6.

workedExamples.title: Roots and order of operations
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Handle the roots first (indices in BIDMAS). √144 = 12 because 12 × 12 = 144, and ∛27 = 3 because 3 × 3 × 3 = 27. Then add: 12 + 3 = 15.

workedExamples.title: Powers of negative numbers
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Indices and root first: (-5)² = 25 (even power, positive), (-2)³ = -8 (odd power, negative), √36 = 6. Then left to right: 25 + (-8) - 6 = 17 - 6 = 11.

workedExamples.title: A tiling problem
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular floor is 144 cm by 108 cm. What is the largest square tile that fits a whole number of times along both sides?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The tile side must divide both 144 and 108, and we want the largest such number – the HCF. 144 = 2⁴ × 3² and 108 = 2² × 3³, so the HCF is 2² × 3² = 4 × 9 = 36 cm. A 36 cm tile fits 4 times along 144 cm and 3 times along 108 cm.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Test 5,624 for divisibility by 8, and test 4,536 for divisibility by 9. Show your reasoning.
workedExamples.solution: 5,624: last three digits 624, and 624 ÷ 8 = 78, so yes. 4,536: digit sum 18, divisible by 9, so yes.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Evaluate (-5)² + (-2)³ - √36 using the order of operations.

activities.title: Build a Human Number Line
activities.materials: chalk or a long strip of paper, and space on the ground.
steps: Mark a line on the ground from -10 to +10 with zero in the middle.
Stand on zero. Call out an integer and step to it – right for positive, left for negative.
Practise addition by walking: to do (-3) + 5, start on -3 and take 5 steps right.
Practise subtraction by turning it into 'add the opposite': for 4 - (-3), start on 4 and step 3 to the right.
Ask a family member to call out sums and race to land on the right answer.

activities.title: Two-Colour Counters (Positive and Negative)
activities.materials: about 30 small objects in two colours – for example light stones for positive and dark stones for negative.
steps: Let each light stone stand for +1 and each dark stone for -1.
A light and a dark stone together make a 'zero pair' and cancel out.
Model (-5) + 3 by laying 5 dark and 3 light stones, removing zero pairs, and reading what is left (-2).
Model 4 + (-6) the same way, and check your answer against the sign rules.
Invent three of your own additions and solve them with the stones.

activities.title: The Integer Card Game
activities.materials: paper cards numbered from -10 to +10 (write them yourself).
steps: Shuffle the cards and deal each player one card face down.
Both players flip their cards over at the same time.
The player whose card is greater wins the round – remember -2 beats -7.
For a harder version, each player flips two cards and adds them; the larger sum wins.
Play until the cards run out, then count who won more rounds.

activities.title: LCM and HCF Investigation
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and a set of small objects to group.
steps: Choose two numbers, for example 8 and 12, and write out the first ten multiples of each.
Ring the first multiple they share – that is the LCM.
Now list the factors of each number and ring the largest they share – that is the HCF.
Check the rule: does LCM × HCF equal the two numbers multiplied together?
Repeat with a new pair and see whether the rule holds every time.

activities.title: Perfect Squares and Cubes Hunt
activities.materials: small square tiles or drawn grid paper, and a few small cubes or boxes.
steps: Arrange tiles into squares: 1×1, 2×2, 3×3, and so on. Record the number of tiles each time – these are the perfect squares.
Explain why the side length is the square root of the number of tiles.
Stack cubes to make bigger cubes: 1, 8, 27, 64. These are the perfect cubes.
Make a wall chart of perfect squares to 15² and perfect cubes to 10³.
Test yourself: cover the chart and write as many as you can from memory.

activities.title: Divisibility Detective (Family Game)
activities.materials: your family or friends, and paper to write large numbers.
steps: One person writes a four-digit number in secret.
Others use the divisibility tests to predict which of 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9 and 10 divide it.
The writer checks each prediction with real division.
Score one point for each correct prediction; the detective with the most points wins.
Swap roles and play again with a new number.

rules.title: Ordering Rule
rules.text: On the number line, further right means greater, further left means smaller. Among negatives, a larger size means a smaller value: -10 < -5.

rules.title: Add and Subtract Rule
rules.text: Same signs: add the sizes and keep the sign. Different signs: subtract the sizes and keep the sign of the larger size. Subtracting a number is adding its opposite: 8 - (-3) = 8 + 3 = 11.

rules.title: Multiply and Divide Rule
rules.text: Same signs give a positive result; different signs give a negative result. Count the minus signs: an even number gives positive, an odd number gives negative.

rules.title: Key rule 4
rules.text: Order of Operations (BIDMAS) Brackets, then Indices (powers and roots), then Divide and Multiply, then Add and Subtract. Divide and multiply rank equally, left to right; so do add and subtract.

terms: A positive whole number, a negative whole number, or zero
The number the same distance from zero on the other side
Distance from zero; always positive or zero
The result of multiplying a number by an integer
A number that divides exactly into another
Lowest common multiple – smallest shared multiple
Highest common factor – largest shared factor
commonMistakes: Same signs give a positive product
LCM = all primes, highest power; HCF = shared primes, lowest power
√25 = 5; two roots only when solving x² = 25

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Evaluate (-5)² + (-2)³ - √36 using the order of operations.
Find √144 + ∛27, and explain which operation you do first and why.
The LCM of two numbers is 120 and their HCF is 8. If one number is 24, find the other.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: LCM × HCF = product, so 120 × 8 = 24 × other, other = 960 ÷ 24 = 40.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Estimate √85 to the nearest integer, showing the two perfect squares you used.
A number leaves remainder 7 when divided by 15. What remainder does it leave when divided by 5? Explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The number is 15k + 7; dividing by 5 gives 3k with remainder 2, since 15 ÷ 5 is exact and 7 = 5 + 2, so the remainder is 2.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Tests for Divisibility.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Divisibility tests are shortcuts that tell you whether one number divides exactly into another, without doing the full division. They save time when finding factors, simplifying fractions, and building prime factorisations. Here are the tests you need for this unit. Divisible by 2: the number ends in an even digit (0, 2, 4, 6 or 8).

questions.question: Round 888,153 to the nearest 10.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 888153 rounds to 888,150 — look at the ones digit.

questions.question: In the number 59,006, what is the value of the digit 9?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 9 is in the thousands place, so it is worth 9,000.

questions.question: Which number is greater: 667,945 or 281,326?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: In the number 22,391, what is the value of the digit 3?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 3 is in the hundreds place, so it is worth 300.

questions.question: Which number is greater: 998,629 or 271,639?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: In the number 40,165, what is the value of the digit 4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 4 is in the ten thousands place, so it is worth 40,000.

questions.question: Which number is greater: 472,569 or 468,939?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: In the number 35,021, what is the value of the digit 2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2 is in the tens place, so it is worth 20.

questions.question: What number comes just after 909,141?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: What does “Integer” mean?
options: A positive whole number, a negative whole number, or zero
The number the same distance from zero on the other side
Distance from zero; always positive or zero
The result of multiplying a number by an integer
questions.answer: A positive whole number, a negative whole number, or zero
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Integer means A positive whole number, a negative whole number, or zero.

questions.question: What does “Opposite” mean?
options: The number the same distance from zero on the other side
Distance from zero; always positive or zero
The result of multiplying a number by an integer
A number that divides exactly into another
questions.answer: The number the same distance from zero on the other side
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Opposite means The number the same distance from zero on the other side.

questions.question: What does “Absolute value” mean?
options: Distance from zero; always positive or zero
The result of multiplying a number by an integer
A number that divides exactly into another
Lowest common multiple – smallest shared multiple
questions.answer: Distance from zero; always positive or zero
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Absolute value means Distance from zero; always positive or zero.

rounds.prompt: In the number 22,391, what is the value of the digit 3?

rounds.explanation: 3 is in the hundreds place, so it is worth 300.

choices: They are equal

choices: A positive whole number, a negative whole number, or zero
The number the same distance from zero on the other side
Distance from zero; always positive or zero
The result of multiplying a number by an integer
rounds.answer: A positive whole number, a negative whole number, or zero
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Integer means A positive whole number, a negative whole number, or zero.

selfAssessment: I can understand that integers include positive whole numbers, negative whole numbers, and zero, and place them correctly on a number line.
I can compare and order integers, and explain why -10 is less than -5 even though 10 is greater than 5.
I can find the opposite of an integer and the absolute value of an integer.
I can add and subtract integers using the number line and the sign rules, including subtracting a negative.
I can multiply and divide integers using the sign rules, and explain *why* a negative times a negative is positive.
I can find multiples and the lowest common multiple (LCM) of two or more numbers.
I can find factors and the highest common factor (HCF) of two or more numbers.
I can apply divisibility tests for 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9 and 10.
